document_info.title: Session Programme: Pressing and Covering to Stop Goals
document_info.topic: Defensive Organization, Pressing Cues, and Recovery Transitions

objectives: Think about when and how to press[cite: 20].
Work with teammates to press and cover[cite: 20].
coaching_points: Get players to think about how their body positioning can help them win the ball[cite: 20]. If they position themselves side on, and stay low, they can better react to the movements of the player on the ball[cite: 20].
Ask players to look for opportunities to challenge for the ball[cite: 20]. A couple of good triggers are when their opponent puts their head down or takes a poor touch[cite: 20].
Encourage your players to steal the ball to set up an attack[cite: 20].

activities.name: Dribble Corridors

pitch_diagram.image_description: Two identical vertical rectangular corridors side-by-side with horizontal lines marking off safe zones at the top and bottom ends[cite: 20]. Blue players are attempting to dribble through the center space while yellow-striped players defend the central gap[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create two 20x15-yard corridors with a safe zone at each end and two defenders in the middle[cite: 20]. The rest start at one end and try to dribble to the other[cite: 20]. If successful, they exit the area and go back to the start[cite: 20]. If a defender steals a ball, they try to get to a safe zone with it to score a point[cite: 20].

activities.name: Stadium 1v1 Keep-Away

pitch_diagram.image_description: Two side-by-side rectangular grids divided horizontally by a halfway line into 'stadium' squares[cite: 20]. Pairs of blue and yellow-striped players are scattered across the quadrants competing for a ball[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create two 20x15-yard areas with a halfway line[cite: 20]. Split players into two teams[cite: 20]. Pair them up with an opponent so everyone's in a 1v1[cite: 20]. Each team has a half as their stadium[cite: 20]. One player in each pair starts with a ball[cite: 20]. Players need to keep it in their own stadium to win a point[cite: 20]. Rounds last 45 seconds[cite: 20].

activities.name: Small-Sided Match Integration

pitch_diagram.image_description: Two parallel small-sided soccer pitches with goals at both ends[cite: 20]. Blue and yellow-striped players are configured in standard match structures with a ball active on the pitch[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create parallel pitches appropriate for the age and stage of your players[cite: 20]. Put a goal at each end[cite: 20]. Players take part in a normal small-sided game on each pitch for ten minutes[cite: 20]. Encourage players to look for opportunities to press and steal the ball[cite: 20].

objectives: Work on when and how to press[cite: 20].
Use speed and body positioning effectively[cite: 20].
Improve their ability to apply pressure to the player on the ball[cite: 20].
coaching_points: Ask players to look for signs to apply pressure, such as their opponent looking down, taking a heavy touch or not having the ball under control[cite: 20].

Emphasise the importance of being able to increase and decrease their speed at the right time[cite: 20]. Defenders need to quickly get close to their opponent but slow down enough so their opponent can't use the defender's momentum against them[cite: 20].
Encourage players to take a side on and lowered position to help them react to the movements of the player with the ball[cite: 20].

activities.name: Standard Small-Sided Game

pitch_diagram.image_description: Two side-by-side green pitches displaying small goals at opposite ends[cite: 20]. Outfield lines indicate a traditional layout where teams are distributed evenly across the pitch area[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create parallel pitches appropriate for the age and stage of your players[cite: 20]. Put a goal at each end[cite: 20]. Players take part in a normal small-sided game on each pitch for ten or fifteen minutes[cite: 20]. Encourage players to think about how and when they could press[cite: 20].

activities.name: Diamond Tag

pitch_diagram.image_description: A square or diamond field boundary with four cones at the perimeter points[cite: 20]. Text overlays point to specific cones showing values for scoring targets: '3 points' at the defender's base cone, and '1 point' at the lateral side cones[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create a 10x10-yard diamond area for this 1v1 game[cite: 20]. Attackers start on one cone with defenders on the opposite cone[cite: 20]. If attackers avoid being tagged, they score a point for reaching a side cone and three points for getting to the defender's cone[cite: 20]. Progress this by giving attackers a ball to run with[cite: 20].

activities.name: End-Zone Restricted Passing Game

pitch_diagram.image_description: A single rectangular field featuring shaded end-zones adjacent to full goals[cite: 20]. Single yellow-striped and blue players occupy the respective defensive zones while the remaining outfielders compete in the middle third[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create an area with a zone and a goal at each end[cite: 20]. Each team has a player in their own defensive zone while the rest are in the middle[cite: 20]. Players work together to score but can't make a backwards pass to a teammate in the middle[cite: 20]. They can pass back to the player in their defensive zone[cite: 20].

objectives: Work on when and how to press[cite: 20].
Improve their ability to apply pressure to the player on the ball[cite: 20].
Dictate the direction of the player in possession[cite: 20].
coaching_points: Once a goal is scored or the ball goes out of play in the 1v1 and 2v2 practices, ensure the next set of players go into the middle[cite: 20]. The original attackers and defenders go off the pitch and swap roles for their next turn[cite: 20].

Ask players to think about their positioning and angle of approach when pressing[cite: 20]. Doing this well can help players dictate what their opponent does next[cite: 20]. For example, forcing a player out wide in the first activity can direct them out of play or towards the side goals which are worth less than the middle goal[cite: 20].

activities.name: Three-Goal Channel 1v1

pitch_diagram.image_description: A vertical grid featuring three small goals or target gates lined up along both end lines[cite: 20]. Text markers indicate scoring rules: '5 points' for the center goal and '1 point' for the outer side goals[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create 15x10-yard areas with three goals at each end for a 1v1 activity[cite: 20]. Have other pairs waiting outside[cite: 20]. The attacker receives the ball and goes for goal[cite: 20]. Scoring in the middle goal is worth five points, the two side goals are worth one point[cite: 20]. The defender can win the ball back and score too[cite: 20]. When a goal is scored, the two players leave, and a new attacker and defender take to the pitch[cite: 20]. The ball gets played to the attacker to start the 1v1 battle[cite: 20]. Meanwhile, the original attacker and defender swap roles the next time they play[cite: 20].

activities.name: Endline End-to-End 2v2

pitch_diagram.image_description: The same grid layout from the arrival activity, but the three mini-goals on the baselines have been removed[cite: 20]. The image shows pairs of blue and yellow-striped outfielders dueling over a ball in the middle sector[cite: 20].

activities.description: Remove the goals from the first activity for this 2v2 practice[cite: 20]. Players look to beat their opponents and dribble past the end line to score[cite: 20].

activities.name: End-Zone Recycled Possession Match

pitch_diagram.image_description: A replica of the week 2 practice 2 diagram[cite: 20]. Outfielders are deployed dynamically in the central block, with passing vectors showing connection patterns back into target players locked inside deep endline fields[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create an area with a zone and a goal at each end[cite: 20]. Each team has a player in their own defensive zone while the rest are in the middle[cite: 20]. Players work together to score but can't make a backwards pass to a teammate in the middle[cite: 20]. They can pass back to the player in their defensive zone[cite: 20].

objectives: Work on when and how to press[cite: 20].
Recognise when they need to delay an opponent[cite: 20].
coaching_points: During the 'part' section of this session, ensure defenders switch roles with the pair of attackers if they've scored in their goal[cite: 20].

Get players thinking about if they can reach the player who is receiving the ball as they take their first touch or not[cite: 20]. If they can't, they need to delay their opponent and position themselves in a way to encourage play in the desired direction[cite: 20].

Emphasise the importance of scanning[cite: 20]. Players need to scan to see what's around them[cite: 20]. Do they, or the closest defender, have cover behind them, or are they isolated?[cite: 20] If they don't have cover, they might need to think about delaying the opponent until more support arrives[cite: 20].
Ask players to look for poor passes, poor first touches and the direction of a first touch to help make the decision to press or delay[cite: 20].

activities.name: Half-Pitch Locked Defenders

pitch_diagram.image_description: A 30x20-yard pitch with a halfway line and full goals at opposite ends[cite: 20]. Attackers move freely across the midline, while defenders without the ball are shown restricted inside one specific half[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create 30x20-yard pitches with a goal at each end and a halfway line[cite: 20]. In this game, the side on the ball can move anywhere[cite: 20]. The side without it are locked in whichever half they were in when they lost possession[cite: 20]. If they win the ball back, they can then move anywhere to attack and score[cite: 20].

activities.name: Tri-Zone Passing Breakout

pitch_diagram.image_description: A field divided evenly into vertical thirds[cite: 20]. Blue attackers occupy the far outer thirds while yellow-striped defenders manage the middle corridor, with one defender stepping out high to press[cite: 20].

activities.description: Split your pitches into thirds[cite: 20]. Have at least two attackers locked in the end thirds and two defenders in the middle[cite: 20]. One defender can go press[cite: 20]. The head height rule is in place[cite: 20]. Attackers work the ball from one side to the other[cite: 20]. If the defenders win the ball, they break out to attack either goal[cite: 20].

activities.name: Whole-Part-Whole Reversion

pitch_diagram.image_description: A reference arrow or diagram pointing back directly to the layout of Practice One[cite: 20].

activities.description: As this is a whole-part-whole session, return to the first practice[cite: 20].

objectives: Work on when and how to press[cite: 20].
Recognise when to apply pressure and when to cover a teammate[cite: 20].
coaching_points: Encourage players to scan to identify who is the closest defender that can apply pressure to the opponent on the ball[cite: 20].
Remind players that as the ball moves, the role of pressing and covering player may change[cite: 20].

Observe players on the line to see if they're scanning the pitch for the position of their teammates and the ball[cite: 20]. This should help them move up and down to cover and protect the goal[cite: 20].

activities.name: Tri-Third Attacker Drive

pitch_diagram.image_description: A vertical pitch divided into thirds with goals on each end line[cite: 20]. An attacker carrying the ball is shown breaking out of an outer third to drive directly into the central defensive grid[cite: 20].

activities.description: Split your pitch into thirds[cite: 20]. Have attackers in the end thirds and defenders in the middle[cite: 20]. Most players are locked in but the attacker on the ball can drive into the middle[cite: 20]. Attackers work the ball from one side to the other[cite: 20]. If the defenders win the ball, they break out to attack either goal[cite: 20].

activities.name: Line Cover and Press 2v2

pitch_diagram.image_description: A field with a single horizontal line containing tall yellow tracking cones[cite: 20]. One defender engages the blue attacker centrally, while their teammate is positioned flat on the cone line to guard the goal mouth[cite: 20].

activities.description: Set up an area for a 2v2 activity[cite: 20]. Have a mini-goal behind a line at each end[cite: 20]. The idea for both teams is to combine to score[cite: 20]. The team that doesn't have the ball works together to always have one player pressing[cite: 20]. The other offers covering support on the line in front of their goal[cite: 20].

activities.name: Screened 4v4 Match

pitch_diagram.image_description: An expanded version of the Practice Two diagram showing an elongated field structure populated by 4v4 player configurations and added horizontal tracking zones[cite: 20].

activities.description: To progress the previous practice, extend the pitch and add another line in for a defender to screen[cite: 20]. Now play the same game but with a 4v4[cite: 20]. Players could start being locked into their areas[cite: 20].

objectives: Know when and how to press[cite: 20].
Understand when to apply pressure and when to cover a teammate[cite: 20].
coaching_points: Time the games to provide high intensity and rest periods[cite: 20]. As a guide you could rotate and mix up teams every eight minutes[cite: 20]. If players are engaged and enjoying it, let the games last longer[cite: 20]. Change it up quicker if players are getting frustrated[cite: 20]. Be aware that two games are 3v3 and one is a 2v2, so you need to work with players to manage the teams[cite: 20].
Ask players to think about their positioning and angle of approach when pressing[cite: 20].
Emphasise the importance of scanning[cite: 20]. Players need to scan to see what's around them[cite: 20].

Ask players to look for opportunities to challenge for the ball[cite: 20]. A couple of good triggers are when their opponent puts their head down or takes a poor touch[cite: 20]. Then encourage your players to steal the ball to set up an attack[cite: 20].

activities.name: Festival Endline Target Game

pitch_diagram.image_description: A compact pitch with dedicated end-zones[cite: 20]. Outfielders duel in a tight central block, utilizing target loops identical to the structural variations practiced in prior weeks[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create an area with a zone and a goal at each end[cite: 20]. Each team has a player in their own defensive zone while the rest are in the middle[cite: 20]. Players work together to score but can't make a backwards pass to a teammate in the middle[cite: 20]. But they can pass back to the player in their defensive zone[cite: 20].

activities.name: Isolated 2v2 Pressure Match

pitch_diagram.image_description: A clear, unrestricted small-sided soccer field with standard goals on the baselines[cite: 20]. A blue player with the ball is directly confronted by a yellow-striped challenger in a 2v2 matchup[cite: 20].

activities.description: Set up a pitch for a 2v2 activity[cite: 20]. This is a normal game[cite: 20]. The focus is on seeing if players can carry out what they've learnt about pressing and covering over the last few weeks[cite: 20].

activities.name: Transition Freedom Match

pitch_diagram.image_description: A field layout with a halfway line and full goals at opposite ends[cite: 20]. Attackers move fluidly across lines, while the team out of possession remains locked in their defensive quadrant until an interception occurs[cite: 20].

activities.description: Create a pitch with a goal at each end and a halfway line[cite: 20]. In this 3v3 or 4v4 activity, the side on the ball can move anywhere[cite: 20]. The side without it are locked in whichever half they were in when they lost possession[cite: 20]. If they win the ball back, they can then move anywhere to attack and score[cite: 20].
